title: Two-mode geometry controls multiscale organization in bipartite systems
authors: Ottavia Falconi, Giulio Cimini, Pablo Villegas
abstract: Many complex systems are organized around complementary roles and naturally described as bipartite networks. Unveiling their multiscale structure presents a fundamental challenge because coarse-graining procedures must preserve role separation, whereas standard approaches collapse it via one-mode projections. Here we introduce a Laplacian-based renormalization framework that operates directly on the bipartite architecture, enabling scale transformations while retaining role differentiation. Using controlled bipartite ensembles at criticality, we show that structural imbalance systematically reshapes organization across scales while leaving scaling properties invariant, revealing a separation between universality and geometry. Applying the coarse-graining framework to empirical bipartite networks, we uncover nontrivial multiscale hierarchies for both roles. In contrast, renormalization performed after one-mode projection -- which truncates diffusion paths to nearest neighbors -- yields qualitatively different structures. Our results identify two-mode geometry as a fundamental constraint for revealing multiscale organization in systems with role separation.
summary: This paper presents a new renormalization method for bipartite networks that preserves their two-mode structure during coarse-graining. It shows that traditional one-mode projections fail to capture the true multiscale hierarchy of complex systems, especially when interactions depend on long-range alternating paths.
main_problem: Standard one-mode projections of bipartite networks collapse the two-mode architecture, leading to a loss of multiscale hierarchical information and a distortion of the underlying diffusion geometry.
main_result: The authors introduce a bipartite-preserving Laplacian renormalization framework (b-LRG) that reveals hidden hierarchies and demonstrates that while structural imbalance reshapes network geometry, it does not alter the universality class of the percolation transition.
method: A Laplacian-based renormalization group (b-LRG) approach using diffusion dynamics on the graph Laplacian, combined with information-theoretic metrics (AMI/NMI) and finite-size scaling analysis.
model_or_system: Bipartite networks with two distinct node classes, including controlled random bipartite ensembles at criticality and empirical networks such as host-virus, plant-pollinator, and social affiliation networks.
key_observables: Giant component size (P_inf), susceptibility (chi), spectral gap function (Psi), spectral specific heat (C), and effective diffusion-mediated distance (D_ij).
important_parameters: Diffusion timescale (tau), partition asymmetry (alpha = N_A/N_B), and average degree (k).
main_assumptions: The renormalization process assumes that clustering can be restricted to nodes within the same class to prevent the creation of artificial intra-class links.
figures_summary: Comparison of standard vs. bipartite renormalization; finite-size scaling of the percolation transition; dendrograms showing hierarchical organization; and information-theoretic comparisons (AMI/NMI) between bipartite and projected networks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of projection-induced distortion, presents the b-LRG framework, analyzes universality and geometry in controlled bipartite ensembles, and validates the framework using various empirical bipartite networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Two-mode-preserving renormalization in bipartite networks. (a), Diffusion-driven coarse-graining in a bipartite architecture. Node classes are identified by different colors. While standard Laplacian renormalization creates mixed blocks, the bipartite renormalization preserves separation between classes. (b), Illustration of a coarse-graining step of the bipartite renormalization scheme, where the scale is increased by integrating fast Laplacian modes. Macronodes are formed independently within each partition, enforcing the absence of intra-class connectivity at all scales. (c), Davis Southern Women network, a prototypical bipartite system of individuals (women) and events they...

Figure 2. Critical scaling and geometry in random bi- partite networks. (a) Giant-component size P∞as a func- tion of the average degree ⟨k⟩(computed on the full bipartite network) for a strongly imbalanced system (α = 0.025) and increasing system size N = NA + NB. (b) Finite-size scal- ing at criticality for different imbalance values α. The maxi- mum susceptibility scales as χmax(kc, N) ∼N γ (main panel), while the order parameter scales as P∞(kc, N) ∼N −β (in- set). Dashed lines indicate the mean-field percolation expo- nents β = γ = 1/3. (c) Susceptibility χ(⟨k⟩, N) as a function of ⟨k⟩for increasing system size. Figure 3. Multiscale hierarchy of virus–host interactions. (a) Global dendrogram of viruses obtained from b-LRG, revealing a hierarchical organization across scales. The outer ring indicates viral taxonomic groups. Large-scale branches do not align with taxonomy, indicating that interaction-derived structure captures an organization distinct from phylogenetic similarity. (b) Intermediate-scale zooms of selected branches. Subtrees exhibit structured clustering associated with shared host environments and interaction patterns (icons), highlighting the emergence of ecological organization at mesoscopic scales. (c) High-resolution views of representative submodules. At small scales, the...

Figure 4. One-mode projection distorts multiscale organization. Adjusted mutual information (AMI) between clusters obtained from the empirical bipartite dendrogram constructed by b-LRG and those derived from degree-preserving bipartite null models (edge-swap CM and BiCM, again constructed by b-LRG) or one-mode projections (unweighted and weighted, constructed by standard LRG), as a function of the number of clusters. We analyze three empirical bipartite systems: host– virus interactions [32], country–product export data [9, 45, 46], and country–language associations [47].
